prevalence.population: Indian population (varies by sanitation and region)
prevalence.source: Parasitology surveillance syntheses in South Asia; WHO intestinal parasite context
prevalence.trend: decreasing overall with sanitation improvements; persistent endemic pockets

incidence.value: Common in endemic areas; outbreaks can occur
incidence.source: Public health surveillance and parasitology studies

burden.dalyPerCapita: moderate in endemic communities due to dysentery and liver abscess
burden.mortalityRate: Low to moderate in severe invasive disease without timely care

vulnerablePopulations: urban slum and rural areas with unsafe water
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Stool testing (antigen/PCR preferred; microscopy less specific), evaluate invasive disease with serology and imaging for liver abscess when indicated

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no routine screening; symptom- and outbreak-driven testing
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good with prompt treatment; reinfection risk persists with poor sanitation

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Microscopy widely used; antigen/PCR limited to reference labs
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Metronidazole/tinidazole for invasive disease plus luminal therapy when available; oral rehydration; sanitation and water safety interventions
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High burden in sanitation-vulnerable areas, diagnostic limitations, overlap with other diarrheal diseases
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Effective low-cost treatments and major prevention gains possible via sanitation programs
